sections.header: Verses 1—10 — The Lame Man at the Temple Gate: Silver and Gold I Have None

heb.paragraph: also translated "Pioneer" or "Prince" — combines the ideas of originator, leader, and champion. The word recurs in Acts 5:31 and Hebrews 2:10; 12:2. The supreme irony: the one who is the source of all life was killed by those he gave life to.

literally "complete strength." The man's restoration is total — not partial mobility but full strength in feet and ankles that had never functioned. Luke's medical precision (as a physician) is evident throughout.

places.name: Gate Beautiful (Nicanor Gate)
places.text: The grand entrance between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel — reportedly made of Corinthian bronze requiring twenty men to open (Josephus, Jewish War 5.5.3). The most trafficked gate of the Temple complex, maximising the healing's witness value.

places.name: Solomon's Colonnade
places.text: The covered eastern portico where Peter delivers his second sermon. A recognised public meeting space used by Jesus himself (John 10:23) and the early church (5:12). The location combines shelter, visibility, and regular audience.

tl.name: First Public Healing
tl.text: Peter heals a man lame from birth at the Temple gate — the first public miracle of the apostolic church. The man's age (over 40, Acts 4:22) makes the healing undeniable and verifiable by the entire Jerusalem community.

refs.note: "Then will the lame leap like a deer" — the messianic promise that the healing enacts. Luke frames the miracle as a visible sign that the eschatological age of restoration has arrived.
"There is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved" — the "name" theology of ch.3 (vv.6, 16) reaches its doctrinal climax in ch.4.
Jesus's healing of a paralytic with the command to "Get up! Pick up your mat and walk" is the direct parallel — Peter acts in the name and pattern of the same Jesus.

notes.note: The ability to heal in the apostolic period was a specific gift given to authenticate the apostles' message before the NT canon was complete. MacArthur distinguishes the apostolic sign-gifts from ongoing spiritual gifts — not to diminish the miracle but to explain why identical miracles are not normative expectations today.

Peter's immediate deflection — "Why do you stare at us as if by our own power or godliness we had made this man walk?" — is the anti-model of religious celebrity. The apostolic pattern is consistent: miracles point beyond the apostle to Christ.

"Times of refreshing" (kairoi anapsyxeōs) — a foretaste of the final restoration. Repentance now participates in eschatological renewal. The call is urgent: the refreshing is available but not permanent.

Calvin notes the bold contrast: "You disowned the Holy and Righteous One" — "God raised him from the dead." The resurrection is the reversal of the unjust condemnation, the divine verdict overturning the human verdict.

The "ignorance" appeal (v17: "I know that you acted in ignorance") is pastoral, not exculpatory. Ignorance does not remove guilt but it does affect the nature of the appeal. Peter does not excuse; he explains — and then calls for repentance. Acknowledging ignorance opens the door to repentance.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Author of life" (archēgos tēs zōēs) — archēgos can mean originator, founder, or pioneer. The irony is maximised: the one who is the source and sustainer of all life was killed — but God raised him. The contradiction is the Gospel's scandal.

"Until the time comes for God to restore everything" (apokatastaseōs pantōn) — the only NT use of apokatastasis. The NET limits its scope to the eschatological fulfilment of the covenant promises made through the holy prophets, not universal salvation.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that "In the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene, walk" uses peripatei — the present imperative: keep on walking. Peter does not pray for healing but commands it — consistent with the authority delegated in the commission of Acts 1:8.

Three verbs in rapid succession: exallomenos (leaping up), periepātei (was walking), ēlthen (entered). Robertson savours Luke's vivid style: the imperfects capture the continuous action of a man who could not stop walking because walking was entirely new to him.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Beggars at temple gates were well-attested in both Jewish and Greco-Roman sources — the socially marginal were drawn to temple precincts where charity was expected. The man's daily position at the Gate Beautiful made him a known fixture, meaning the healing would be recognised and verifiable by the entire Jerusalem community (Acts 4:16).

The economic contrast is embedded in Peter's words: no silver or gold (the currency of the Temple economy) but the authority of the name. The healing demonstrates that the church's power is not the power of wealth or institution but the power of the risen Christ.

hist.context: The healing of the lame man is Luke's first post-Pentecost miracle — and he describes it with three vivid verbs in v8 (jumping, walking, praising God) that enact Isaiah 35:6's messianic promise: 'then will the lame leap like a deer.' The miracle is not just a sign of power but a sign of the age: the eschatological restoration Isaiah promised has begun. Peter's immediate deflection (v12: 'Why do you stare at us as if by our own power?') models the apostolic pattern throughout Acts — miracles point beyond the apostle to the risen Christ.

sections.header: Verses 11—26 — Peter’s Sermon in Solomon’s Colonnade

heb.paragraph: aorist imperative — decisive, urgent turning. This is the fourth major repentance call in Acts (2:38; 3:19; later 17:30; 26:20). The pattern is consistent: the Gospel proclamation always concludes with a call for decision.

designated beforehand — the appointment was before history, not merely after the resurrection. Jesus was divinely designated for this role from eternity; the Ascension publicly declares what was always true.

places.name: Gate Beautiful (Nicanor Gate)
places.text: The grand entrance between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel — reportedly made of Corinthian bronze requiring twenty men to open (Josephus, Jewish War 5.5.3). The most trafficked gate of the Temple complex, maximising the healing's witness value.

places.name: Solomon's Colonnade
places.text: The covered eastern portico where Peter delivers his second sermon. A recognised public meeting space used by Jesus himself (John 10:23) and the early church (5:12). The location combines shelter, visibility, and regular audience.

tl.name: Moses — The Prophet Promise
tl.text: Moses promised Israel a "prophet like me" (Deut 18:15). Peter declares the promise fulfilled in Jesus — the greatest of all the prophets who succeeded Moses.

tl.name: Peter's Sermon — Temple
tl.text: Peter's second sermon in Solomon's Colonnade draws crowds to faith. The covenantal argument — you are heirs of the prophets — makes Israel's acceptance of Jesus the fulfilment of their own heritage.

refs.note: The prophet-like-Moses text applied to Jesus — also cited in John 1:21; 6:14; Stephen's speech (Acts 7:37); and Hebrews 3:1–6.
"Through your offspring all peoples on earth will be blessed" — the Abrahamic promise Paul develops in Galatians 3:16, 29. The Gentile mission is covenant fulfilment, not covenant abandonment.
Stephen repeats the Deuteronomy 18 application to Jesus — confirming it was a standard piece of early Christian apologetic, not a one-off application.

echoes.source_context: Moses promised that God would raise up a prophet like himself from among the people — one to whom Israel must listen. This prophet would speak God's words with Moses' authority.
echoes.connection: Peter identifies Jesus as that prophet: God raised up his servant and sent him to bless you. Stephen makes the same identification in his speech: Moses spoke of the prophet to come.
echoes.significance: The prophet-like-Moses expectation shaped messianic hope. Jesus is not merely another prophet but the prophet — the definitive revealer who speaks God's final word.

echoes.source_context: God promised Abraham that through his offspring all families of the earth would be blessed. This universal promise bracketed the particular election of Israel.
echoes.connection: Peter quotes this to the Jerusalem crowd: 'You are the heirs of the covenant God made with your fathers.' The blessing promised to Abraham now comes through his descendant Jesus.
echoes.significance: The Abrahamic promise of universal blessing provides scriptural grounding for Gentile inclusion. The particular (Israel) always pointed toward the universal (all nations).

notes.note: MacArthur notes that the acknowledgment of ignorance does not diminish responsibility; it explains why repentance is still available. Had Israel committed a fully informed, deliberate sin with no possible excuse, the situation would be hopeless. The ignorance means the door remains open.

MacArthur takes "restore everything" as referring to the prophesied kingdom restoration for Israel — the fulfilment of OT covenant promises. He connects this to the disciples' question of 1:6 (restoring the kingdom to Israel): Jesus said the timing was the Father's; Peter says it awaits the national repentance of Israel.

Calvin interprets "restore everything" not as universal salvation or political restoration but as the completion of the redemptive plan — when all God's elect are gathered and his promises to the church are fully consummated.

Calvin uses this passage to press the priority of the Jewish mission: "first to you" shows that the Gospel is not the abandonment of Israel but the fulfilment of Israel's covenant destiny. The Gentile inclusion does not mean Jewish exclusion; it means the Abrahamic promise has reached its universal scope.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Who has been appointed for you — even Jesus" (ton prokecheirismenon hymin Christon Iēsoun) — prokecheirismenon means "designated beforehand." The appointment was pre-temporal: Jesus was divinely designated for this role from eternity, not merely appointed after the resurrection.

"Raised up his servant" (anastēsas) — this verb can mean either raise from the dead or raise up/appoint. In v26, "sending him to you" suggests appointment/commissioning rather than resurrection per se. Luke uses the same verb for the resurrection in v15.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: Robertson notes that "times of refreshing" (kairoi anapsyxeōs) is a phrase not found elsewhere in biblical Greek. He connects it to Jewish expectation of the messianic era as a time of rest and renewal — the call to repent is therefore not merely moral but eschatological.

The "must listen" (akousesthe) is an imperative of obligation. The "cutting off" echoes covenantal excommunication (karet — being cut off from the people of God). Peter applies this warning with full seriousness: rejecting Jesus is covenant-breaking.

keener.source: Craig S. Keener, Acts: An Exegetical Commentary (4 vols., 2012–2015) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The "acting in ignorance" appeal fits Jewish penitential theology: ignorance (shegagah) was a recognized category affecting the nature of atonement required. Peter's pastoral move — acknowledging ignorance before calling for repentance — is consistent with Jewish homiletic practice: soften the accusation to open the door to repentance.

Peter's identification of his audience as "heirs of the prophets and of the covenant" gives them covenantal standing that makes their rejection of Jesus all the more urgent to address — but also makes their repentance all the more possible. The covenant membership can now lead them to receive the blessings Jesus brings.

hist.context: Peter's second sermon constructs a deliberate typological argument: Abraham's covenant promise → Moses's prophet-like-me prophecy → Jesus. Each element points forward to its fulfilment. The Deuteronomy 18:15 citation ('a prophet like me') was a live messianic expectation in the first century (John 1:21; 6:14; 7:40). Peter applies it with full covenantal seriousness: Israel's history pointed to Jesus, whom they rejected — and yet, because of Israel's covenantal standing, the first offer of repentance is made to them.

ppl.name: Peter↗ People
ppl.role: Lead apostle; spokesperson for the Jerusalem church
ppl.text: Peter (Simon bar Jonah) dominates Acts 1–12 as the church’s primary spokesman and miracle-worker. He preaches at Pentecost (Acts 2), heals at the Beautiful Gate (Acts 3), defends before the Sanhedrin (Acts 4–5), raises Tabitha (Acts 9:36–42), and receives the vision that opens the gospel to Gentiles (Acts 10). His release from Herod’s prison by an angel (Acts 12) closes his narrative in Acts; Paul’s story takes over from ch.13.

ppl.text: Peter's immediate deflection (v12: ' The ability to heal in the apostolic period was a specific gift given to authenticate the apostles' message before the NT canon was complete…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThen Peter said, \ESVBut Peter said, “I have no silver and gold, but what I do have I give to you. In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, rise up and walk!”NIVOne day Peter and John were going up to the temple at the time of prayer \u2014 at three in the afternoon.ESVNow Peter and John were going up to the temple at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews (c.93 AD)
src.quote: Josephus provides extensive first-century background for the political, religious, and social conditions described in Acts — the Herodian family, Pharisees and Sadducees, Roman provincial governance.
src.note: Acts’ historical details consistently align with Josephus’s independent account of the same period, supporting the narrative’s historical credibility.

src.title: Dead Sea Scrolls (1QS, 1QM)
src.quote: The Qumran community texts illuminate the diversity of Second Temple Judaism from which Christianity emerged — their eschatological expectations, community rules, and temple criticism parallel aspects of the early church.
src.note: The Scrolls demonstrate that the early church’s practices (community meals, shared goods, eschatological expectation) were not unprecedented within Jewish renewal movements.

src.title: Greco-Roman Travel Literature and Epistolary Conventions
src.quote: Acts’ travel narrative (ch.13–28) conforms to the conventions of ancient Greek travel literature, and Paul’s speeches follow rhetorical models documented in Greco-Roman oratory.
src.note: The Hellenistic literary conventions confirm Acts’ composition for a sophisticated Greco-Roman audience and support its historical reliability as ancient historiography.

rec.who: John Chrysostom, Homilies on Acts (c.400 AD)
rec.text: "Chrysostom’s 55 homilies on Acts are the most thorough patristic commentary, emphasising the Spirit’s active role and the apostles’ moral example for the church."

tx.title: Western text (Codex Bezae D) vs Alexandrian text
tx.content: Alexandrian(P45, P74, א, B) is the primary scholarly text.Western(Codex Bezae D) is 10–15% longer, with additions that appear to expand and harmonise.
tx.note: Acts has the most significant NT textual variation. The Western text of Acts is not simply corrupt but may preserve early oral expansions. Most scholars follow the shorter Alexandrian text as more original.

tx.title: P45 and early papyrus witnesses
tx.content: The Chester Beatty Papyrus (P45, c.250 AD) is the earliest substantial Acts manuscript, generally supporting the Alexandrian text with some unique readings.
tx.note: The papyrus evidence has largely confirmed the Alexandrian tradition as the best text of Acts, though the Western text’s substantial additions remain a subject of scholarly investigation.

debate.title: Authorship and Historicity of Scripture Walkthrough

positions.name: Traditional Lukan authorship (Hengel, Keener, classical scholarship)
positions.proponents: Attributes Acts to Luke the physician (Col 4:14), a companion of Paul, on the basis of the "we" passages, historical accuracy, and early church tradition.
positions.argument: Luke’s medical vocabulary (noted by Hobart) and the detailed geographical and political accuracy of Acts support the traditional attribution.

positions.name: Anonymous/secondary authorship (Haenchen, Conzelmann)
positions.proponents: Questions Lukan authorship on the basis of discrepancies between Acts’ Paul and the Pauline letters, suggesting a later author using Lukan sources.
positions.argument: This view notes that Acts does not always match Paul’s letters precisely, and argues the "we" passages may be a literary device rather than eyewitness markers.

coaching.tip: 'Silver or gold I do not have, but what I do have I give you' (v. 6). Peter offers something better than coins: 'In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk.' The lame man leaps. The apostles continue Jesus' ministry.

'Heaven must receive him until the time comes for God to restore everything' (v. 21). Jesus reigns from heaven; restoration awaits his return. Peter links Jesus to Moses' prophecy (Deut 18): the Prophet who must be heard.
